question_title: What will the WastewaterSCAN national wastewater category for Norovirus GII be for the week ending June 13, 2026?
summary: The WastewaterSCAN national category for Norovirus GII was MEDIUM as of May 30, 2026, with no clear 21-day trend. However, multiple news articles from May 28-29 reported the national category was HIGH, indicating rapid oscillation near the HIGH/MEDIUM boundary. For the week ending June 13 (2 weeks later), three competing forces shape the outcome: (1) strong seasonal decline favoring Low (norovirus peaks in winter, declines to summer trough by mid-June; concentrations down 31% April→May; CDC outbreaks at 19/wk heading to 4-9/wk), (2) status quo persistence favoring Medium (flat 21-day trend, experts say levels are "not unusual"), and (3) boundary volatility allowing a High rebound (was HIGH just days before the question was written, 50 of 147 sites still at HIGH). Medium is the most likely single outcome given the flat recent trend and 2-week horizon, but the seasonal signal gives Low meaningful probability, while the very recent HIGH reading keeps High plausible.

factors.description: Status quo is Medium as of May 30, with no clear 21-day trend - status quo tends to persist over short 2-week timeframes
Strong seasonal decline pattern: norovirus typically at summer trough by mid-June; CDC outbreaks declining from 78/wk peak to 19/wk toward 4-9/wk baseline
National category was HIGH just 2-3 days before May 30 reading (per NBC/Newsweek articles citing WastewaterSCAN), indicating boundary oscillation
May WastewaterSCAN blog: concentrations down 31% month-over-month (April to May), clear declining trajectory
Late-season elevated activity with 50/147 sites still at HIGH; Midwest and Northeast showing medium-to-high activity
Experts say levels 'broadly consistent with historical averages' and 'not unusual' - normal seasonal tail, not anomalous surge
GII.17 variant share declined from 74.5% to 19.6%, removing the anomalous driver from 2024-25 season
Dashboard operational reliability: 99% detection rate, robust national sampling network makes 'Not Calculated' very unlikely

reasoning:
**Forecast: WastewaterSCAN Norovirus GII National Category (Week Ending June 13, 2026)**

| Outcome | Probability |
|---------|-------------|
| **Medium** | **40%** |
| **Low** | **32%** |
| **High** | **23%** |
| **Not Calculated / Other** | **5%** |

**Key reasoning:**

- **Medium (40%)**: The status quo as of May 30 is Medium with a flat 21-day trend. With only 2 weeks until resolution, inertia favors persistence. Experts describe current levels as "not unusual" for this time of year.

- **Low (32%)**: The seasonal signal is very strong — norovirus historically reaches its summer trough by mid-June. Concentrations declined 31% from April to May, and CDC clinical outbreaks are heading toward baseline. The GII.17 variant driver has faded.

- **High (23%)**: The national category was HIGH just 2-3 days before dropping to Medium, showing the system is oscillating near the boundary. 50 of 147 sites remain at HIGH, and regional activity (Northeast, Midwest) could push the national average back up.

- **Not Calculated (5%)**: Very unlikely given 99% detection rate and robust sampling, but accounts for remote dashboard issues.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) What will the WastewaterSCAN national wastewater category for Norovirus GII be for the week ending June 13, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43752)
[Vomiting virus is spreading across most of the U.S.](https://www.nbcnews.com/health/health-news/norovirus-gastrointestinal-symptoms-spreading-us-wastewater-rcna347108)

[Highly Contagious Norovirus Rising Across the U.S. as Experts Warn of Persistent Spring Surge](https://yournews.com/2026/05/29/7011735/highly-contagious-norovirus-rising-across-the-u-s-as-experts-warn/)
[Map reveals where norovirus is hitting hardest in US](https://www.newsweek.com/norovirus-map-shows-where-vomiting-virus-levels-are-high-across-us-12011290)
resolution_criteria:
This question resolves based on the national "Wastewater Category" shown for Norovirus GII (target "Noro_G2") on the [WastewaterSCAN national dashboard](https://data.wastewaterscan.org/) when viewed for the National location, reflecting data through the week ending Saturday, June 13, 2026. The question will be resolved using the dashboard reading taken between June 14 and June 17, 2026 (whichever first reflects samples through the week ending June 13, 2026).

- "Low" resolves if the dashboard's national status label for Norovirus is "low".
- "Medium" resolves if the label is "medium".
- "High" resolves if the label is "high".
- "Not Calculated / Other" resolves if the dashboard shows "Not Calculated," "Not Detected," or no category is displayed for the national Norovirus GII view.

If the WastewaterSCAN dashboard is unavailable, the corresponding WastewaterSCAN monthly nationwide update post on https://wwscan.ghost.io/ covering June 2026 will be used as a fallback. If neither source provides a national Norovirus GII category by June 17, 2026, the question resolves as "Not Calculated / Other."
fine_print: If WastewaterSCAN renames the category labels (e.g., "Moderate" instead of "Medium"), the equivalent middle-tier label resolves as "Medium." Only the national-level reading is used; regional readings are ignored.

factors.description: Status quo is Medium as of May 30, with no clear 21-day trend - status quo tends to persist over short timeframes
Strong seasonal decline pattern: norovirus typically reaches summer trough by mid-June; CDC outbreak data declining from 78/wk peak to 19/wk
National category was HIGH just 2-3 days before dropping to Medium (late May articles), indicating boundary oscillation and volatile readings
May WastewaterSCAN blog: concentrations down 31% month-over-month (April to May), clear declining trajectory
Late-season elevated activity: multiple news sources report 'sustained transmission' and unusual late-spring norovirus spread, 50 of 147 sites still at HIGH
Experts quoted say levels are 'broadly consistent with historical averages' and 'not unusual' - this is not a true anomaly, just normal seasonal tail
GII.17 variant declining (74.5% → 19.6%), removing the anomalous driver that extended last season
The 21-day trend being 'no clear trend' (flat) rather than declining suggests the seasonal decline may have stalled temporarily
Dashboard operational reliability: 99% detection rate, robust sampling network - very unlikely to show Not Calculated

input.anchor: Historical seasonal pattern: In mid-June, norovirus levels are typically at their annual nadir. In a normal year, by week ending June 13, we'd expect Low. But the current starting point is Medium (not yet Low), and recent HIGH readings suggest elevated persistence this season.
input.assessment:
The national WastewaterSCAN category for Norovirus GII was HIGH as recently as late May but dropped to MEDIUM by May 30. Two competing forces shape the next 2 weeks:

PRO-DECLINE (toward Low): Strong seasonal pattern favors continued decline into summer trough. Concentrations fell 31% from April to May. CDC clinical outbreaks at 19/week heading toward historical June baseline of 4-9/week. GII.17 variant declining.

PRO-PERSISTENCE (toward Medium): Status quo is Medium with flat 21-day trend. The recent HIGH→MEDIUM transition suggests levels are on the boundary. 50 of 147 sites still at HIGH. News reports describe "sustained transmission." Experts say this is normal for the time of year, not anomalous, suggesting gradual rather than rapid further decline.

PRO-REBOUND (toward High): Was HIGH just 2-3 days ago. Multiple news articles from late May describe norovirus as actively spreading. Northeast seeing rising rates. Boundary oscillation makes a flip back to HIGH plausible.

Two-week timeframe: Enough for meaningful change but not dramatic shifts. The most likely single outcome is Medium (status quo persistence with flat trend). Low is possible if seasonal decline accelerates. High is plausible given the recent boundary oscillation.
input.calibration_notes: The seasonal base rate would favor Low (~50%+), but the current Medium status with flat trend and recent HIGH readings argues against Low being dominant. My estimate shifts weight from Low toward Medium and High relative to the seasonal base rate. I'm not at 50% on any option, reflecting genuine uncertainty across three plausible outcomes.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Whether the recent HIGH→MEDIUM transition reflects the start of rapid seasonal decline or just noise. 2. Whether the late-season 'sustained transmission' pattern persists into mid-June. 3. The exact position of current concentrations relative to tertile boundaries. 4. Whether 2 weeks is enough for meaningful further decline from the current plateau.
input.tool_audit: Useful: research() provided CDC NoroSTAT data. fetch_url successfully extracted WastewaterSCAN May blog data (MEDIUM, 31% decline, site breakdown) and NBC/Newsweek article details. web_search found relevant late-May news coverage. Empty/Failed: Could not directly access WastewaterSCAN interactive dashboard (JavaScript-rendered). get_metaculus_questions confirmed question details but no community prediction available.
input.update_triggers: A dashboard check in early June showing Low would strongly shift toward Low resolving. Continued HIGH readings would shift toward High. News of a new outbreak surge would shift toward High.
input.process_reflection: The biggest gap was inability to access the live WastewaterSCAN dashboard directly - it's JavaScript-rendered and the fetch tool only got navigation elements. The blog posts and news articles served as good proxies. The contradiction between news articles (HIGH nationally) and the question description (MEDIUM as of May 30) was initially confusing but ultimately very informative - it revealed the system is oscillating near the HIGH/MEDIUM boundary. Having access to the raw wastewater concentration data (rather than just categorical labels) would enable much more precise forecasting.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

### Hallucinated evidence — Minor issue (non-impactful)
The research() tool incorrectly stated WastewaterSCAN uses category labels "minimal, low, moderate, high, very high" (trace lines 87-88, 102, worldview research entries). The actual categories are "Low, Medium, High" as confirmed by the WastewaterSCAN May 2026 blog fetch and the question's resolution criteria. However, the agent's final factors and assessment correctly use the real "Low/Medium/High" labels — the erroneous research output did not contaminate the forecast.

### Double-counting — Mild
- **Factors 1 and 8** both support Medium and both draw on the same "no clear 21-day trend" observation from the May 30 dashboard. Factor 1 frames it as "status quo persistence" while Factor 8 frames it as "stalled seasonal decline." This is the same evidence repackaged.
- **Factors 2 and 4** both support Low and draw on overlapping decline evidence (seasonal pattern + 31% MoM drop), though they address different time horizons.
- **Factors 3 and 5** both support High and overlap on elevated activity evidence (recent HIGH reading + 50 sites at HIGH).

None of these individually distort the forecast, but the cumulative effect is mild factor inflation on all sides.

### Wrong-direction factors — None found
All logit signs align with their descriptions. Factor 9 (negative logit for Not Calculated) correctly pushes against that outcome given the 99% detection rate.

### Contradictory assessment — None found
The assessment reaches a Medium-favoring conclusion (42%) consistent with the factors collectively. The factor-implied model suggested Low at 44.5%, but the agent explicitly justified departing from this by noting the flat trend and recent boundary oscillation. This is a reasoned override, not an inconsistency.

### Missing resolution criteria — Not an issue
Resolution criteria are provided and the agent engages with the specific terms (WastewaterSCAN national dashboard, Noro_G2 target, Low/Medium/High categories, week ending June 13).

### Resolution misalignment — None found
The agent's factors correctly focus on the national-level category from WastewaterSCAN, matching the resolution criteria.

### Counterargument quality — Good
The counterargument substantively argues for Low being underweighted, provides specific data (19/wk → 4-9/wk baseline, 31% MoM decline, experts dismissing the "surge"), and challenges the High probability separately. This is a genuine alternative story, not a token gesture.

### Confidence assessment — Appropriate
0.35 confidence is well-calibrated given the high uncertainty about dashboard boundary dynamics, the inability to access the live dashboard, and the 2-week forecast horizon.

### Probability assessment — Slight concern but within bounds
The agent's estimate (H: 26%, L: 27%, M: 42%, NC: 5%) underweights the seasonal signal relative to the evidence. Key considerations:

1. **The seasonal decline is very strong evidence**: Norovirus follows one of the most predictable seasonal patterns in infectious disease. By mid-June, levels are historically at their nadir. Concentrations fell 31% April→May. CDC clinical outbreaks at 19/wk heading toward 4-9/wk baseline.

2. **The agent's own factor-implied model gives Low ~44.5%** — the agent overrode this by 17.5pp based on narrative judgment about boundary oscillation and flat trend. The counterargument explicitly argues Low should be 40-50%.

3. **High at 26% seems somewhat elevated**: A return to HIGH would require reversal against the seasonal grain. The HIGH→MEDIUM transition happened because of declining concentrations. "Sustained transmission" ≠ "rising transmission." Experts say the pattern is normal.

My independent estimate: Low ~33%, Medium ~40%, High ~20%, NC ~7%. This differs by ~6pp on Low and ~6pp on High. The overall direction is the same (Medium most likely), and the differences don't clearly meet the threshold for "meaningfully wrong" — the agent is within a defensible range, just leaning a bit too much on the recent HIGH reading and not enough on the strong seasonal signal.

### Tool audit — Consistent
All claims match the ground-truth metrics: 6 fetch_url calls (0 errors), 2 web_search calls (0 errors), 1 get_metaculus_questions call (0 errors), 1 research call (0 errors). The dashboard JavaScript limitation is confirmed by the trace.

### Cross-question consistency — No contradictions
The related forecast (#43751 on CDC NoroSTAT cumulative outbreaks) is consistent: it describes the same seasonal decline trajectory and late-spring decline pattern. No contradictory assumptions.

### Worldview research contradictions — Minor
The worldview research entries infer WastewaterSCAN category should be "low or minimal" in early June, while the agent's actual forecast favors Medium (42%). This is not a true contradiction because the research entries explicitly note they couldn't access the dashboard and were working from clinical outbreak proxies — the agent's later research (fetching the blog and news articles) corrected this inference with actual WastewaterSCAN data showing Medium.

### Overall verdict: WARN
The mild double-counting across multiple factor pairs and the slight underweighting of the seasonal signal (Low should arguably be ~5-6pp higher, High ~5-6pp lower) warrant a warn but not a fail. The forecast direction is correct, the evidence chain is well-grounded, and the research was thorough within the constraints of an inaccessible JavaScript dashboard.
